title: On the notion of genus for division algebras and algebraic groups
speaker: Andrei Rapinchuk
abstract: Let $D$ be a central division algebra of degree $n$ over a field $K$. One defines the genus gen$(D)$ of $D$ as the set of classes $[D']$ in the Brauer group Br$(K)$ where $D'$ is a central division $K$-algebra of degree $n$ having the same isomorphism classes of maximal subfields as $D$. I will review the results on gen$(D)$ obtained in the last several years, in particular the finiteness theorem for gen$(D)$ when $K$ is finitely generated of characteristic not dividing $n$.

proof_stages.label: Maximal-subfield question and motivations
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates the problem of relating central division algebras that have the same maximal subfields, deriving it from length-commensurable Riemann surfaces and contrasting it with Amitsur's theorem on common splitting fields.

proof_stages.label: Genus of a division algebra
proof_stages.summary: The genus of a central division algebra is defined as the set of Brauer classes represented by division algebras with the same maximal subfields, leading to the two guiding questions of when it is trivial and when it is finite, with number-field and quadratic-form benchmarks.

proof_stages.label: Stability over rational function fields
proof_stages.summary: A stability theorem says that trivial genus for quaternion algebras, and more generally for exponent-two algebras, passes from a field to its rational function field, ultimately relying on unramified Brauer-group control.

proof_stages.label: Large-field construction of nontrivial and infinite genus
proof_stages.summary: By iteratively adjoining function fields of suitable quadrics, one can keep chosen quaternion algebras nonisomorphic while forcing their maximal subfields to match, and with more bookkeeping obtain infinite genus over very large fields.

proof_stages.label: Ramification control and finiteness over finitely generated fields
proof_stages.summary: For finitely generated fields and degree prime to the characteristic, the genus is finite because algebras with the same maximal subfields have the same ramification at discrete valuations and the relevant unramified Brauer torsion is finite.

proof_stages.label: Genus for algebraic groups via maximal tori
proof_stages.summary: The definition is extended from maximal subfields to maximal K-tori of absolutely almost simple algebraic groups, with number-field finiteness/triviality results, conjectural finitely generated-field analogues, and partial results for norm-one, spin/unitary, and G2 groups.

proof_stages.label: Good reduction as group-theoretic unramifiedness
proof_stages.summary: Good reduction at a discrete valuation is introduced as the analogue of an unramified division algebra, and the key theorem shows that all groups in the genus inherit good reduction, reducing finiteness of genus to finiteness of forms with good reduction almost everywhere and yielding local-global consequences.

proof_stages.label: Purely transcendental base change and killing the genus
proof_stages.summary: Purely transcendental extension adds no new genus classes from the base, but sufficiently many independent variables can collapse the genus completely for type G2 and to the minimal Brauer-theoretic ambiguity for norm-one groups of division algebras.

examples.name: Length spectra of hyperbolic Riemann surfaces
examples.note: The speaker presents locally symmetric spaces, especially Riemann surfaces, as the setting in which the authors first met the maximal-subfield question: length commensurability forces many common quadratic subalgebras of the associated quaternion algebras.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker constructs the associated quaternion algebra for a Fuchsian quotient and traces how closed geodesic lengths relate to eigenvalues and maximal subalgebras

examples.name: Cubic counterexamples to a finite-extension Amitsur analogue
examples.note: The discussion of Amitsur's splitting-field theorem and cubic central simple algebras over number fields shows why maximal subfields cannot simply recover the Brauer subgroup and motivates measuring the residual ambiguity by a genus.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker outlines the local-invariant construction of cubic counterexamples by ramifying at places with invariants ±1/3 and shuffling them

passes.reason: The cubic division algebras are explicitly introduced as counterexamples to a finite-extension analogue of Amitsur’s theorem, but structurally they are ordinary members of an infinite family.

examples.name: Quaternion algebra killed by one transcendental variable
examples.note: The final quaternion special case illustrates the general killing theorem: after adjoining one independent variable, even a quaternion algebra with infinite genus acquires trivial genus.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the general n−1 transcendence-degree killing result to quaternion algebras, deriving that one variable kills the genus

passes.reason: A quaternion algebra becoming genus-trivial after adding one transcendental variable illustrates the killing theorem, but a quaternion algebra is not a sporadic, degenerate, extremal, or maximally symmetric object here.
An ordinary quaternion algebra is used to show that base change to one purely transcendental variable can kill rather than preserve genus elements.
